seo.title: Bangkok Travel Guide: Everything You Need to Know (2026)
seo.metaDescription: The complete Bangkok travel guide for 2026. 4-day itinerary, transport tips, best food, etiquette rules, budget breakdown and everything else you need to plan a perfect trip to Thailand's capital.

intro: Bangkok is one of the world's most visited cities — and for good reason. In four days you can immerse yourself in 700 years of Thai Buddhist culture, eat your way through Michelin-starred street food, explore a labyrinth of ancient canals and end your evenings at rooftop bars 60 floors above the city. This guide distils the essential Bangkok experience into a practical, honest four-day framework that works for first-timers and returning visitors alike.

itinerary.title: 4 Days in Bangkok: The Essential Itinerary

days.title: Royal Bangkok — Temples, Palaces & the River
activities: 08:30 — Grand Palace & Wat Phra Kaew: Start with Bangkok's most iconic landmark. Buy your 500 THB ticket at the gate, dress code applies (cover shoulders and knees).
11:00 — Wat Pho: Walk 700 m south to see the 46-metre Reclining Buddha and get a traditional Thai massage at the on-site school (420 THB, 30 min).
12:30 — Lunch at Krua Apsorn: A beloved old-school Thai restaurant near the Grand Palace famous for crab omelette and crab curry — budget 120–200 THB per dish.
14:00 — Cross to Wat Arun by ferry (5 THB from Tha Tien pier) and climb the porcelain-encrusted prang for river panoramas.
16:30 — Chao Phraya Express Boat north to Phra Arthit Pier and walk into the Banglamphu neighbourhood for sunset drinks.
19:00 — Khao San Road: street food, Chang beer at 80 THB, and the backpacker circus. Walk side streets Soi Rambuttri for a calmer vibe.

days.title: Chinatown, Markets & Bangkok's Food Soul
activities: 07:00 — Lumpini Park: Start early with a morning walk among tai chi practitioners and monitor lizards before the heat builds.
09:30 — Jim Thompson House: Take a guided tour of the silk tycoon's extraordinary Thai-house museum near BTS National Stadium (200 THB).
12:00 — MBK Shopping Centre or Siam Paragon: Explore Bangkok's legendary malls around Siam BTS station for lunch and air-conditioned browsing.
15:00 — MRT to Hua Lamphong, then walk into Chinatown's Sampeng Lane for the afternoon gold shop district and hardware market atmosphere.
18:00 — Wat Traimit: See the 5.5-tonne Golden Buddha before Yaowarat Road lights up.
19:30 — Chinatown dinner: T&K Seafood for grilled prawns, or queue at Jay Fai (Michelin star) on Maha Chai Road for the famous crab omelette (book weeks in advance).
21:30 — Night market stroll: Continue along Yaowarat Road sampling mango sticky rice, oyster omelettes and Chinese doughnuts.

days.title: Markets, Canals & Rooftop Bangkok
activities: 07:30 — Arrange a day trip to Damnoen Saduak Floating Market and Maeklong Railway Market (leave hotel by 07:30 for an organised tour). Budget 1,200–1,500 THB including transport.
09:00 — Damnoen Saduak: Paddle through the canal market, sample coconut pancakes and mango sticky rice from boat vendors.
11:30 — Maeklong Railway Market: Watch stall umbrellas fold dramatically as a real train passes through the market.
13:30 — Return to Bangkok and lunch near your hotel.
15:30 — Chatuchak Weekend Market (Saturday or Sunday only): 2 hours shopping for ceramics, vintage clothing and antiques.
18:30 — Return to hotel to change (smart casual for rooftop).
20:00 — Vertigo Rooftop Bar at Banyan Tree Hotel, 61st floor: cocktails from 550 THB with 360° city views. Book a table in advance.

days.title: Thai Cooking Class & Neighbourhood Bangkok
activities: 09:00 — Thai cooking class with market tour: 4-hour session learning 4–5 dishes starting with a morning market visit to buy fresh ingredients. Budget 1,200–1,800 THB.
14:00 — Afternoon free: explore the Thong Lo (Sukhumvit Soi 55) neighbourhood for Bangkok's best independent cafes and local street food.
16:00 — Wat Benchamabophit (Marble Temple) if not yet visited — particularly beautiful in afternoon light.
18:00 — Rooftop or riverside drinks: Sky Bar at Lebua State Tower for the Hangover 2 experience, or the rooftop at SALA Rattanakosin for a view of Wat Arun at dusk.
20:00 — Final dinner: Jodd Fairs Night Market (Thursday–Sunday, MRT Thailand Cultural Centre) for local Thai night market energy, or Nam restaurant at COMO Metropolitan for elevated Thai fine dining.

transport.fromBangkok: Bangkok is Thailand's main transport hub. Suvarnabhumi Airport (BKK) serves international and long-haul flights; Don Mueang (DMK) handles domestic and budget airline routes. From Suvarnabhumi, the Airport Rail Link (45 THB, 28 min) runs to Phaya Thai BTS station. Metered taxis cost 250–400 THB to Sukhumvit depending on traffic; always use the official metered taxi queue on the arrivals floor. From Don Mueang, bus A1 (30 THB) connects to Mo Chit BTS, or take a metered taxi (300–450 THB to Sukhumvit). Domestic flights connect Bangkok to Chiang Mai, Phuket, Koh Samui, Krabi and all Thai regional airports multiple times daily. Hua Lamphong (MRT) is Bangkok's main train station with overnight services to Chiang Mai (12–14 hr, 600–1,500 THB) and Hat Yai (south Thailand).
localTransport: BTS Skytrain: The elevated metro covering Sukhumvit (east-west) and Silom (north-south). Single journeys 16–59 THB; Rabbit Card stored-value card avoids queuing. Runs 05:30–00:30.
MRT Metro: Underground network covering the Old City ring, Yaowarat, Silom, Sukhumvit interchange and the northern Chatuchak/Bang Sue areas. Fares 17–42 THB. Runs 05:30–00:00.

Chao Phraya Express Boat: River ferry with stops at all major temple piers (Maharaj, Tha Chang, Pha Athit). Orange flag boats 15 THB per journey; tourist boats 60 THB all-day pass. Best way to reach riverside temples without traffic.
Grab: The Uber of Southeast Asia. Download before arrival; works for taxis, private cars, motorcycle taxis and food delivery. Price-transparent and cashless — major advantage over street taxis.
Metered taxis: Flag-fall 35 THB, most cross-city rides 80–200 THB. Drivers speak limited English — show your destination written in Thai characters for best results.
Tuk-tuks: Iconic three-wheeled auto-rickshaws; negotiate price firmly before boarding. Short trips 80–150 THB. Fun for first-timers, overpriced for regulars.
Khlong (canal) boats on Khlong Saen Saep: Fast east-west connections from Banglamphu to Pratunam and beyond — 10–20 THB, very local experience.

mustTry: Pad Thai — stir-fried rice noodles with egg, bean sprouts, shrimp and ground peanuts. Try Thip Samai on Maha Chai Road for the definitive Bangkok version (100–150 THB).
Tom Yum Goong — hot and sour shrimp soup with lemongrass, galangal and kaffir lime leaves. Explosive flavour; order it at any sit-down Thai restaurant (150–280 THB).
Som Tum — green papaya salad pounded in a mortar with fish sauce, lime, chilli and dried shrimp. Spicy, sour and addictive; 50–80 THB at street stalls.
Massaman curry — slow-cooked curry with Persian influence: cinnamon, cardamom, potato and peanuts in a rich coconut sauce. Best at sit-down restaurants (150–250 THB).
Mango Sticky Rice (Khao Niao Mamuang) — glutinous rice with fresh mango and warm coconut cream. Best April–June when Nang Dok Mai mangoes are in season; 80–120 THB.
Crab omelette (Kai Jeow Poo) at Jay Fai — Michelin one-star street food legend on Maha Chai Road. Jumbo crab omelette is 1,000+ THB but worth every baht.
Guay Jub — rolled rice noodle soup in a peppery broth with crispy pork; a Chinatown speciality at Nai Ek Roll Noodle (80–120 THB).
Boat noodles (Kuay Teow Rua) — intensely dark, rich noodle broth originally cooked and sold from boats. Victory Monument area has Bangkok's best concentration (40–60 THB per small bowl).
Grilled seafood at T&K Seafood, Yaowarat — whole grilled fish, prawns and crab with seafood sauce at street-side tables in Chinatown (200–600 THB per dish).
Roti with condensed milk — flaky pan-fried flatbread with banana, condensed milk and chocolate sauce at street stalls near Khao San Road (40–70 THB).
foodAreas: Yaowarat (Chinatown) — Bangkok's most celebrated food street, blazing with neon from 19:00. Seafood, dim sum, Michelin-starred street food and Chinese desserts.
Victory Monument area — Boat noodle alley, green papaya salad stalls and cheap local rice-and-curry shops in the shadow of the central roundabout.
Chatuchak Market food section — Vast selection of regional Thai dishes, grilled meats and fresh coconut ice cream inside the weekend market.
Thong Lo (Sukhumvit Soi 55) — Bangkok's hippest neighbourhood with independent cafes, Japanese-Thai fusion restaurants and excellent craft cocktail bars.
Silom Soi 20 — Lunchtime food stalls serving office workers; massaman curry, rice dishes and Thai desserts at 50–80 THB per plate.
Jodd Fairs Night Market (Ratchada) — Contemporary night market with street food, live music and a young Thai crowd; open Thursday–Sunday from 17:00.
tips: Eat where Thais eat — plastic chairs and no English menu is usually a good sign. Google Translate's camera function can decipher Thai menus in seconds.
Most street food stalls open from 07:00 (breakfast noodles), 11:00 (lunch rice dishes) or 18:00 (evening grills and seafood). Many close by 22:00.
Always ask 'pet nit noi' (a little spicy) or 'mai pet' (not spicy) — Thai chilli levels are genuinely extreme by Western standards.
7-Eleven and FamilyMart convenience stores are everywhere and sell surprisingly good sandwiches, rice balls and hot food for 20–60 THB — great for late-night snacks.
Street food hygiene: cooked-to-order dishes are very safe. Avoid pre-cut fruit in direct sunlight and salads rinsed in tap water at less reputable stalls.

etiquette: The Monarchy: Never make critical or disrespectful comments about the Thai royal family — it is a criminal offence under the lèse-majesté law (Section 112) carrying up to 15 years imprisonment for foreigners.
Temples: Remove shoes before entering any temple building. Cover shoulders and knees — skirts, sarongs and long trousers are appropriate. Do not point your feet at Buddha images or monks.
Monks: Women must never touch a monk or hand objects directly to one. If you wish to give something to a monk, place it in front of him or on a cloth he holds out.
Wai greeting: The Thai wai (hands pressed together at chest level, slight bow) is a sign of respect. Return a wai when greeted — a slight nod and smile also suffices for foreigners.

Head and feet: The head is considered the most sacred part of the body in Thai culture; never touch anyone's head, even a child's. Feet are considered the lowest — never point them at a person, Buddha image or the entrance of a house.
Public displays of affection: Holding hands is accepted; kissing in public is considered rude by older Thais, particularly near temples.
Bargaining: Expected at markets, street stalls and with tuk-tuk drivers. Always smile and remain good-humoured — aggressive bargaining is seen as rude. A 20–30% reduction is usually achievable.
Tipping: Not obligatory but appreciated. Round up taxi fares; leave 20–50 THB at casual restaurants; 100–200 THB at mid-range restaurants; 10% at upscale venues.
Shoes off: Always remove shoes when entering a Thai home or guesthouse room, and at many small shops and restaurants that display a shoe rack at the entrance.

budget.budget: 800–1,500 THB per day (~23–43 USD): Dorm bed 300–500 THB, street food meals 40–150 THB each, BTS/MRT transport 30–80 THB/day, temple fees 100–500 THB each. Very comfortable on 1,200 THB/day if you eat locally.
budget.midRange: 2,500–5,000 THB per day (~71–143 USD): Private hotel room 2,000–4,000 THB, sit-down restaurant meals 250–600 THB each, occasional taxi use, one paid activity per day. This is the sweet spot for most independent travellers.
budget.luxury: 8,000–20,000+ THB per day (~229–571+ USD): 5-star riverside hotel, fine dining, private tours, rooftop bars (500–900 THB per cocktail), spa treatments. The Mandarin Oriental or Peninsula pushes daily spend well above 15,000 THB before extras.
